---
title: "CellShard Behavior"
---

CellShard presently functions as the storage and execution-staging substrate beneath Cellerator rather than as a general analytical environment. The active ingest and preprocess path now treats MosaiCell as the native preprocessing owner: MosaiCell computes the CellShard-layout QC, normalization/log1p metrics, keep masks, and raw-count validation contract, while Cellerator's workbench surface remains a compatibility wrapper around orchestration, metadata rewrite, and finalize calls. From that point forward, CellShard assumes responsibility for the durable representation of that matrix, for the derivation of execution-facing layouts from it, and for the controlled publication of those derived artifacts to the readers and executors that consume them.

The central distinction in its behavior is the separation between canonical persistence and hot execution. The `.csh5` container is the durable source of truth. It holds the authoritative dataset header, partition and shard boundaries, payload arrays, and the runtime-service metadata needed to identify generations and ownership. The `.cspack` artifacts derived from that container are not archival objects; they are execution artifacts. CellShard materializes versioned CSPACK generations from `.csh5`, serves those generations for repeated multithreaded access, and treats them as the fast path for host fetch, delivery, and downstream staging. In practical terms, `.csh5` remains the stable canonical body of the dataset, whereas `.cspack` carries the transient but operationally decisive form in which that dataset is read at runtime.

This distinction is reinforced by an owner-hosted service model that persists even when the entire workflow resides on a single machine. Canonical file access is intentionally narrow. One owner-side coordinator governs generation identity and publication state; one master reader retains responsibility for canonical `.csh5` access during hot operation; pack-preparation workers materialize or refresh execution generations; executor threads or nodes consume those published generations rather than mutating the canonical source in place. Executor-facing reads are expected to fail closed when the required published pack is absent rather than reopening `.csh5` and rebuilding execution state ad hoc. The absence of a network hop in single-machine mode does not relax the contract. It merely collapses roles that remain logically separate: canonical ownership, pack preparation, and execution still occupy distinct positions in the runtime.

In distributed operation the same contract is extended rather than replaced. The owner host retains the canonical `.csh5`, prepares the relevant pack generation, and routes CSPACK data to remote executors. Executor nodes may retain delivered CSPACK artifacts in node-local caches, but those caches remain operational conveniences rather than sources of truth. The authoritative dataset is not redefined by the location of a hot copy. It remains anchored to the owner-hosted canonical container, while the published pack generation defines the current readable execution epoch.

CellShard's live behavior is therefore governed by publication and cutover rather than by in-place mutation. Reads proceed against versioned CSPACK generations. Append staging may occur concurrently, but new material does not become part of the active runtime view until a new generation has been prepared and explicitly published. Canonical payloads are append-only during hot reads; overwrite or relocation is reserved for explicit maintenance conditions. This gives the system a precise temporal structure: immutable canonical generations, separately prepared execution generations, and a service epoch that determines which prepared state is currently visible to readers.

The practical consequence is that CellShard should not be understood as a thin file wrapper around sparse payloads. It is a runtime discipline for keeping canonical storage, derived execution state, and reader visibility rigorously separated. Cellerator hands over either a newly ingested matrix or a MosaiCell-produced keep-mask plus compatibility-layer metadata rewrite. CellShard persists the canonical result, derives execution-facing layouts from it, publishes those layouts as CSPACK generations, and preserves a narrow ownership boundary around the canonical source while local or remote executors consume the published result.

For Blocked-ELL specifically, the current ingest and finalize posture is now storage-first rather than block-occupancy-first. Single-cell matrices can make a `32`-row row block look deceptively dense at the block level because almost every feature block appears somewhere inside that row block, even when scalar occupancy is still sparse. The active Blocked-ELL tuner therefore minimizes padded scalar-value bytes first and only then uses block-union fill as a secondary tie-break. The default candidate set also includes `4x4` blocks so sparse omics data can avoid the pathological full-width row-block unions that made earlier optimized Blocked-ELL payloads far larger than their CSR-like lower bound.

The ingest path is still GPU-first for COO-to-Blocked-ELL conversion, but it no longer trusts that CUDA build blindly. After each GPU-built canonical part returns, Cellerator now validates the live nonzero count against the source COO semantics before the part becomes authoritative. If the GPU builder produces a payload whose live entries do not match the source view, ingest drops back to the CPU builder for that part rather than emitting a `.csh5` whose matrix header and optimized blocked shard body disagree. This keeps the storage contract correct while the remaining CUDA-side layout bug is isolated and fixed.

Blocked-ELL now follows the same contract shape as sliced execution storage: a normal blocked `.csh5` persists only the optimized shard blobs under `/payload/blocked_ell` together with the matrix header, partition metadata, shard offsets, and execution metadata. It does not store a second heavyweight canonical `/payload/blocked_ell` body beside that execution-form payload. Older files may still carry the legacy `/payload/optimized_blocked_ell` group and remain readable, but new blocked writes normalize onto the plain blocked payload path. Canonical blocked partitions are reconstructed lazily only for compatibility surfaces that still ask for canonical semantics. In other words, shard-addressable optimized payload is the durable blocked representation, and canonical blocked is a derived compatibility view rather than a second on-disk source of truth.

Those optimized shard blobs now also compact their reorder metadata instead of serializing the full remap surface redundantly at every layer. Shard-level column permutations are stored once, inverse maps are reconstructed on load, partition-local row permutations are stored as a single direction when possible, and the on-disk permutation width narrows to `u8` or `u16` whenever the local row or column count permits it. This does not change the semantic contract of the remap tables, but it cuts metadata overhead inside optimized blocked shards and makes the persisted execution form closer to the actual sparse payload rather than a sparse payload wrapped in duplicate `u32` bookkeeping.

The same separation now applies to user-defined metadata. Observation annotations, feature annotations, and dataset-level user attributes are stored canonically inside `.csh5`, but they are not part of the default hot execution bootstrap. Owner snapshots advertise whether those cold metadata surfaces exist, and reader threads or remote clients fetch them explicitly when they need semantic context. This keeps axis annotation expressive without turning CSPACK delivery or shard-local transfer into an annotation-heavy path.

The same owner-side cold path now also covers manual subset and reorder materialization. When a caller wants an ordered row selection, an ordered feature selection, or a manually grouped output, CellShard treats that as a derived materialization request rather than as a hot logical view. It gathers the requested rows and features from canonical storage, rewrites the selected annotations and provenance into the requested order, rebuilds a new optimized Blocked-ELL payload, and can warm a reusable CSPACK cache for that derived result. In other words, ad hoc reorder and subset operations remain Cellerator-facing at the selector stage, but the physical rewrite and CSPACK publication stay inside the CellShard storage/runtime boundary.
